topic: The minimal-facts method for the resurrection
framing: When someone objects that the resurrection can't be argued because it just rests on the Bible, the strongest reply is the 'minimal facts' method (Habermas and Licona): don't ask the sceptic to grant the Gospels' reliability — build only on a few facts that even critical, non-Christian scholars already grant, then argue that the resurrection is the best explanation of them. It is an inference to the best explanation, the way a historian weighs rival hypotheses, never a mathematical proof. The honest limit to concede openly: it removes the HISTORICAL excuse for unbelief but cannot by itself compel someone whose worldview rules out miracles in advance. As Dale Allison presses, how one weighs the probabilities depends heavily on the priors one brings — which is precisely why the argument's force is to shift the burden onto that anti-miracle assumption, not to leave the resurrection a matter of taste. The argument's real power is that it leans on what the critics themselves grant. For the full case with sources, point to the Evidence Library's minimal-facts essay.

topic: The early 1 Corinthians 15 creed and its dating
framing: For the resurrection, lead with the early creed, not 'the Gospels say so.' In 1 Corinthians 15:3-7 Paul quotes a fixed tradition he says he 'received' and 'delivered' — that Christ died, was buried, was raised, and appeared. Critical scholars across the spectrum date this creed to within a few years of the crucifixion (roughly 2-5 years); the stronger 'within months' claim should be attributed to those who argue it (e.g. James Dunn), not asserted in our own voice. That early dating is the whole point: it is far too early for legend to have done its slow work, which answers the 'it was written decades later' objection. Keep the conclusion modest — 'the disciples sincerely believed they saw the risen Jesus,' never 'it is proven they saw him.' For the scholarship, point to the creed/dating essay.

topic: The empty tomb — the case and its honest limits
framing: The empty tomb is a genuine strand of the case, but the SOFT one — present it as such, not as bulletproof. Three honest supports: the resurrection was proclaimed in Jerusalem, where opponents needed only to produce the body and did not; the earliest Jewish counter-claim ('the disciples stole him') itself concedes the tomb was empty; and all four Gospels make women the first witnesses, which an invented story in that culture would not have done. The honest limit to state plainly: the empty tomb is granted by fewer scholars than the crucifixion or the disciples' experiences, and the often-quoted 'about three-quarters of scholars' figure is Habermas's own tally of the literature he has read, not an audited poll — so never inflate 'a majority' into 'virtually all.' Concede the observation, never more than the evidence gives. For detail, point to the empty-tomb essay.

topic: Hallucination and vision theories of the appearances
framing: 'They hallucinated' is the objection serious critics actually press, so answer its strongest form, don't caricature it. At its best (Bart Ehrman) it is not lone hallucination but shared bereavement or religious visions — like reported group apparitions. Two things resist that: the witnesses include people with no grief and no expectation — Paul, a hostile persecutor, and James, a sceptic who had thought Jesus was out of his mind — whereas grief-visions arise among those already longing for the one they lost; and no such vision tradition has ever produced a community convinced a particular person was bodily raised and then willing to suffer and die for it. Keep the claim modest and accurate: hallucinations are normally individual, and it is the CONJUNCTION of facts that resists a natural account — not a flat claim that group visions are psychologically impossible. For depth, point to the essay.

topic: The conversions of Paul (enemy) and James (sceptic)
framing: Two conversions carry unusual weight because they are hostile witnesses, not friendly ones. Paul was actively persecuting the church — not grieving, not expecting a messiah — when he became convinced he had seen the risen Jesus; he converted on what he took to be first-hand evidence, not someone else's testimony. James, Jesus' own brother, was an unbeliever during the ministry (an embarrassing admission the Gospels would have no motive to invent) yet became a leader of the Jerusalem church and, by non-Christian attestation (Josephus), was martyred. Neither man fits the profile a grief-hallucination or wish-fulfilment theory requires. Keep it to 'became convinced he had seen him' / 'sincerely believed,' never 'proven.' Concede their sincerity and the facts, never the sceptic's frame. For the sources, point to the Paul-conversion essay.

topic: Did Jesus really die? (the swoon / apparent-death theory)
framing: 'Maybe Jesus only fainted and later revived' (the swoon theory) turns on a distinction worth making explicit: it GRANTS that Jesus was crucified — which is about as well-attested as anything in ancient history, conceded even by hostile sources (Josephus, Tacitus) and by sceptics like John Dominic Crossan, who calls the crucifixion 'as sure as anything historical can ever be' — and disputes only that the crucifixion actually killed him. That is the implausible part. Roman crucifixion was a reliable method of execution, and the idea that a severely flogged, crucified man could survive it, escape a sealed tomb unaided, and then persuade his followers he had risen as the Lord of life asks far more of the evidence than simply concluding he died. Handle the physical specifics soberly, not gruesomely, and don't lean the whole case on any single medical study. For the detail, point to the essay.
